All right, well as people start to continue to show up, maybe give a quick overview of the format today. So joining me on the call, obviously, Andre and I, and then Lauren will be joining as well, Lauren. And as I've mentioned several times in comments, sort of prior to the traffic engine and the manifesto, and then within the comments in the academy, Lauren has worked with me for a long time. He is far closer to the tactical side, the implementation side of Facebook than I am, really knows the buttons to push, the levers to pull. And Lauren is that rare combination of people who can do all three skills that Facebook requires really well, strategy side, on the button pushing side, sort of setup side, and also in the copywriting side, the ad copy creation. And it's rare to find someone who does all three, so I feel very fortunate to have Lauren on the team. And he's just a great guy too. In three days, yeah. The money comes running out. All right, I'm going to start with, this is a set of questions all from one person. And I will not read the person's name just for their privacy, and so I don't make a fool of myself and mispronounce some of the names. So this is a series of questions from one person. The traffic engine is really, my goal for it, my dream vision for it, is that it is useful for someone who has not advertised before, and it's just getting started, giving you a 10 or 12 year head start on avoiding a lot of mistakes, and knowing what to focus on. And then for people who have already run traffic, it's a way to think about doing that differently and without the need to focus on the mechanics. So please don't ever feel bad about asking questions. This is complicated stuff. So I appreciate the courage to raise your hand and ask a question that seemed uncomfortable, but I'm asking you sort of as a friend, please don't feel uncomfortable to ask questions. You are far more representative of more people in this first cohort than you recognize, and it takes courage for someone to raise his or her hand and say, I'm one of the people that has this question, a lot of us aren't really asking it, so thank you for that. One of the challenges with any how-to course in tech is that it changes so quickly. So there will be an over my shoulder, and probably if I haven't asked him to do this yet, but hey, Lauren, probably an over Lauren's shoulder, some parts as well. What we wanna be careful about though is whenever possible, we show the how-to stuff by linking to Facebook specific and Google specific instructions, just because when things change and they change very quickly, we want to not have something confusing in a screencast or anything else. So we'll find a balance there. Now getting all the way back to the original question, e-commerce. And I'm curious to hear Lauren's take on this too. My take on e-commerce is pretty straightforward. If you're selling a commodity that somebody can get anywhere, anytime, and there's no real story behind the product, I think it's very difficult to sell it on Facebook because why? I mean, unless you're the absolute cheapest or there's some comparative advantage, sure. But where I think Facebook excels is where you can begin to tell the story of a product. So it wouldn't be the same kind of long form ad. And I don't think you need to try to fit it into that, but you can tell the story of how the product came to be. Like what was the inspiration? You know, I was hiking in this mountain and my gear broke and I knew I was frustrated or whatever. And that started a three year journey to create the ultimate XYZ, whatever. That's something to engage somebody's interest. Not just a, hey, here's this thing. Because again, Facebook traffic, they're not actively looking, no one's searched. No one's trying to find your thing. So when they see it, we need to kind of come in sideways in a way that seems conversational and is interesting. And it's attractive, but what makes it attractive about the product is the story around the product or the story of how you found the product. Lots of ways to come at this, but that the central, the common denominator on all of them from my perspective for e-commerce is that there is a story worth reading that a prospect can become interested in. And that pulls them toward the product to be a little bit more interested in it. Sometimes that can be about, you know, a founder's story, something like sure. But a lot of the times it's more of a, here's something unique about the product. Like one of the examples I gave early on was a way, travel, one of their bags, you know, the thing that resonated for me in their advertising is they talked about the lifetime warranty on wheels, which those are the things that break. And that to me just, I thought that was interesting. And then I read more about the story of how they developed the product. And it was fascinating, but I couldn't just go buy that thing anywhere. And I could buy a travel bag anywhere, but I was brought into their world because of the story of how they came up with this product. And I think for e-commerce, that's a really, it's a foundational concept. Now, having said that, Lauren, what's your take on e-commerce? Yeah, I think a lot of it depends on the product. And, you know, I'm very utilitarian with Facebook. So if something works, it works. If you're selling t-shirts, you may not, you may be all right, just putting out a very image-based ad of, hey, I've got a t-shirt that baseball fans will love. And that's all you've got to do. That may work best. It may not. A story, you know, to Sean's point, a story that talks about what is different about this t-shirt. Why would, you know, the creator's story, the creation of the product, the benefits, whatever you can talk about to just incite some engagement and some interest, that may be a better way to go. One thing too is video. So there's more than one way to skin a cat. And I don't want to represent that there's one be on, end all way for e-commerce or really for anything. But the more complicated a product is, the more that you really want to differentiate your product. You know, as Sean was saying, so that it's not like a commodity. If you're talking about mushroom coffee, for instance, you know, that may be a great time to have a story. May have a great time to have a long format which informs people about the benefits and what exactly mushroom coffee is. The same philosophy and the same principle of long form coffee can be applied in video. It could be the same kind of pre-qualifying principle which just happens in the medium of video. Those may be very effective for products that might not be visual, you know, an image of mushroom coffee would probably not do as well as a long format explaining it. So I would encourage you to try whatever you think kind of in your gut will work and tweak as needed. And particularly if it's a visual product, you may be okay. Doing a little more image related ads. And then to the earlier question about what to optimize for, purchases. You would want to ideally optimize for purchases. That may not be feasible depending on your budget and your cost per purchase. You may not be getting enough purchases on a daily or weekly basis so that Facebook has enough data to optimize properly. But if you can, purchases is where I would start and what I would envision being your most effective optimization. Thanks, Lauren. Lauren mentioned mushroom coffee. Andre and I were talking about that earlier and it just, it made me think of a way to approach this. So there's a company called Forcigmatic. They're sort of pioneers in this medicinal mushroom consumable space, fascinating story. But I didn't know their story. I heard it tertiary early and kind of consume the product. But I happened to listen to a podcast with the founder and what the founder, it was just a strange name. Like Forcigmatic, it's odd. That's sort of one of those things you wonder like, where did that come from? But when the founder explained it, it was such a fascinating story. I'm like, why don't you tell this story in your ads? Because Forcigmatic is based on this Forcigmas of data that gets you to the point at which you find the 50 most studied foods, most studied, I think I guess foods, most studied foods in history. So among them are mushrooms, certain types of mushrooms. You know, things that are like, they're like red ginseng and green tea extract. And there's a small group of 50 of the most studied compound or study, I guess it's just foods, we'll say, studied foods over time. And that's what the company focuses on. I thought, well, that's a really interesting story. Like that would be a great ad. Like our company was founded on this idea that there are 50 of the most studied foods in human history over thousands of years and that can't be an accident. So that's what we focus on. That's a wonderful story for sort of a weird product. So just, you know, I like the story part. Toloran's point, which I think is always important to remember, it starts simply. Like don't try to imagine, you know, a 1500 word journey of a story to a product that you could just sell by saying, hey, I have this thing and it's great for these three reasons, you might be interested. If you can sell doing that, just do that. Don't make it more complicated than it needs to be. So there's a spectrum and start simply. And then if that, if simple isn't quite working, then layer on what you think might be working. It's much easier to layer on and see the impact that it is to start huge and then pull things away and try to judge what happened. It's just a very different experience. Much harder to assess that. One way you can do that too, is to pay attention to the comments. The comments may only add, people may have specific questions, they may have specific objections. They may occasionally just, you know, insult your product for some reason. And part of long form copy might be that you address those specific objections. You know, mushroom coffee, like is it safe? Well, okay, is it legal? I mean, you know, these may be, is it related to, you know, the magical mushrooms, as we talked about? Like these may be questions that your prospect has as you can answer with a copy as well. It's not only just telling a story, but you have the chance to just kind of create a well-rounded sales argument. All right, let's move on to the next one. Actually, before we move on, you open the door to comments, Lauren. I wrote about this in one of the modules, I really wanna draw everybody's attention to this just because I don't know what happens to people when they're in Facebook, but some percentage of people turn into monsters. And you're going to see comments, when you put yourself out there with advertising on Facebook, you're going to see comments that are mind-numbing. Like, and I just, it never ceases to amaze me the depths of depravity that people will go through in their comments about things that they just seem viscerally offended by an ad showing up in their news feed. Lauren has sort of mentioned how to handle that as far as not deleting them, but just steal yourself for the possibility that you're going to read things in comments that you're going to take very personally, don't. But it's a weird phenomenon, I don't get it. I think Facebook would benefit from having some mechanism to address that. But until they do, just let it roll off your back. All right, let's move on to the next question. This is a long series of questions. So I think I'm going to go through all of it and then we'll kind of come back to it. So Lauren, when we do that, I might come back and ask you some specific parts just because I'm reading, and you have this question too, so you can refer to it. All right. I'm running a number of Facebook campaigns, but I wanted to focus on just one of them for my question. It is a broad Facebook campaign to cold traffic. No layering, no interests, no lookalikes, no custom audiences. I excluded all previous customers and subscribers. I uploaded their emails as lists in Facebook to this rather than use the pixel data. My reasoning for doing this is what you refer to in module five as Facebook's black box data. So quoting me from module five, when you advertise on Facebook, you're getting black box access to this vast amount of data. You can't interact with it directly, but it informs the ways in which Facebook identifies prospects and how the Facebook algorithm optimizes advertising on your behalf. As I have run numerous past campaigns, my reason my conversion pixel has already been primed with the correct profile data for Facebook to figure out the correct people to show my ad to, people likely to buy what I offer. I know it breaks the rules, but the AI appears really, really smart. Going broad seems to be the way moving forward, providing you have primed the conversion pixel. Thoughts on priming the pixel, am I deluded? I only target the UK mobile devices, male, female, age between 35 to 64. I optimize for offsite conversions. I pay for impressions. My bid strategy is lowest cost without cap. Since May 1st until May 18th, I have spent is that pounds, 1,637 pounds and gross 4,005 pounds selling a collection of 15 PDFs for 10 pounds each, netting me 2,368 pounds in profit. Don't laugh, I know that's chicken feed to you guys. Since May 1st, I have sold 248 front end and 61 upsells. The upsell costs an extra 25 pounds, 25.6% take rate for the upsell. Question, it currently has a frequency of just over two, excuse me, and conversions, which are purchases are slowing down. How do I scale the campaign? I can't figure out how to ramp this up. I'm also currently using layered targeting, which is working okay. I have three layered interests. Also burn through the lookalikes. 1% lookalike was great. 1% to 2% was okay. 2% to 3% broke even. What is your advice? Many thanks as always. So before we get into this, this is very detailed questions. Lauren, we can sort of approach this a couple of ways. We can, you can riff on it as much as you want. I'll start with a couple of high level things that I'm interested in. And I think we can take some of this, we can bring some of this into an actual module, maybe build on some of this, or we can, we'll figure out how best to do that. The one place where I want to start is, the line don't laugh. I know that's chicken feed to you guys. I want everybody to hear the sentence before that, right? From May 1st to May 18th, he spent 16, 1,637 pounds and gross 4,005 pounds netting a profit of 2,368. In no world is that chicken feed. Is no world, is that something to be diminished? When you have a success like that, that is never laughable. That is to be celebrated. I hope you are incredibly proud of yourself for that because you've done the thing that is incredibly hard to do. You made a profit. You made a profit on Facebook advertising. Despite all the hoopla that you read about how easy it is, it is not easy. It's exceptionally difficult and you did it. That's the whole thing. And congratulations, seriously, that's amazing. Just hearing that success is absolutely amazing. So having said that and ignoring all of the really difficult questions in here, Lauren, do you want to come at this at different angles? How do you want to approach answering this? There's a lot of detail in here. Yeah, I can go ahead and answer a lot of these questions. So first of all, yeah, to XO Sean, I totally 100% agree. A thousand pounds a week is nothing to laugh at. That's amazing. Particularly too, as PDFs you're selling, it's a cold traffic. Man, yeah, I should be taking lessons from you. That's awesome. To answer your question about broad audience targeting, yes, that can be very effective targeting. Just broad audience, no targeting, no, sometimes people select demographics, but just go broad. That can be very effective. As you mentioned, that happens once your pixel has enough data that it can do that. That it understands kind of the profile of the person who purchases. So you mentioned priming the pixel. Yeah, I don't think of it as priming or like there's some threshold you have to get to to make it work. It is just a continual stream of data that comes into the pixel and the more the better. At what points are you able to go broad? No idea. It's really just one of those things you're going to have to try and see what happens. As far as you mentioned, uploading customer lists and email signups as opposed to using your pixel data, there's pros and cons to either of those approaches. The advantage of using an uploaded list is that you can create a custom audience that is durable. So you can create an audience of purchasers, and that audience will stick around for years. That's not going to change. You'll be able to continually use that audience. When you use pixel data, it has a time limit on it. So I think it's a maximum of 180 days that you can. If someone has that, the purchase pixel is fired, you can put them in an audience for a maximum of 180 days, which means you could exclude them for 180 days. Once they get to 181 days, they're no longer excluded based on that pixel data. So that's the advantage of the uploaded lists. The disadvantage of the uploaded lists is that what Facebook is going to do is they're going to look at the first name and the last name and the phone number and the email and all the information that you give them from your customer list, and they're going to attempt to match that to a profile. In order to exclude that Facebook profile from your ads, that process is imperfect. And sometimes, depending on the quality of data that you've received, you may match 90%. You may match 80%. You may match as little as 50%. So that is the disadvantage of those lists. The pixel data is perfect. The pixel data, you will get that one-to-one matching. So use both. I mean, there's really no reason why you couldn't use both. And I, in fact, often do. You mentioned a frequency over 2. And the upcoming module, actually, which Sean and I are working on now, we get a little more into the metrics and the specific reporting numbers. But frequency is one to watch. And you're right. Once you get to about 2 over a certain time period, you'll notice that a lot of times your performance will drop off. Again, there's no magic threshold. It's not like 1.9 is going to work. And all of a sudden, you get to 2.0 and it falls apart. But once you start to get there, keep your eyes open. And you may notice the performance dips. And it sounds like it has for you. The cure for that is just to refresh your creative. To new copy, new images. You can do that with a minimum of effort by changing your images and changing your copy that is above where the C-more click, the intro copy. Because that's what people are seeing when they're scrolling through their feed. So you could real quickly just take your long copy and adjust the intro paragraph, put in a new image, and have a new ad ready to go. So refreshing your creative is hopefully not that difficult. Then as far as how to scale, you mentioned that you had burned for your lookalikes. That's not really something you burned through. If you're refreshing your creative, you can keep going back to the well. You can run ads indefinitely to your lookalike 1% audience because you are refreshing your creative. And they're seeing different ads every single time. The same with any audience, not just lookalikes. As far as how to scale, one simple way is just to add budget. And that's what most people think. That's minimally effective because what's going to happen is as you add budget, your cost per acquisition inevitably is going to go up. So that can get you a little ways. And it's a tool in your toolbox. But it's not everything. Most people, what I would recommend is just going to different audiences. You mentioned broad targeting. If you're already there, it's kind of difficult to get a different audience from that. But if you're running a lookalike 1% of purchasers, maybe you're going to 1% lookalike of your leads. Or people who have visited your website in the last 30 days. Or maybe instead of a 1%, you run a 2% or a 3%. 5%, 8%, 10%. You can go up to 10%. Try different audiences. You can target different interests. You can select different layers. Lots of ways that you can creatively go find different pools of people to put your ad in front of. Audience Insights, I think I've talked about that in module class. Audience Insights is a great tool for doing that because you'll notice some strange things. People who, if I'm running an ad for testosterone supplementation or for male anti-aging, a lot of those guys are into motorcycles, which kind of makes sense if you think about it. But that would not be your first intuitive targeting option. But through Audience Insights, you can discover that. And then plop that in as an audience and use that. And that's the way to scale. So I want to draw attention to one thing you said. It's obviously all of it was brilliant, but I really want to hone in on one part of that. It's easy to think in terms that are very complicated. So it's like, OK, we have an ad that's hit a certain frequency. And now that we're there, that ad is no longer the resource that we thought it was. Because we don't want to keep showing it. So we need the complicated things. We got to go back to the beginning and create a new ad. And to Lauren's point, no, you don't. We know that those first two lines of an ad are, that's the high leverage point. So simply changing those out, swapping out an image, those two things that take maybe 15 minutes, you've got a new ad. You've sort of reset the clock. And if you're thinking in terms of, well, how did the ad that I have been running, what fears, what needs, what desires was that directed toward? Sort of what emotions was I trying to elicit with that? Now let me come at it a little bit differently. Because this is a large audience. And the thing that you came at it with one way got you a certain result. But now you have this opportunity. And this speaks to scale as well. That when you're within a sort of finite audience size, that to come at it from a different way. Because a Facebook audience is interesting in that. It can be enormous. But at any given point in time, really somewhere between 5%, 7%, maybe 10% of that audience is even remotely likely to engage with what you have the way you've described it. But the beauty of it is that 5% to 10% changes over time. And you can also affect that change by coming at things with a little bit different approach. And you don't have to reinvent a 1,900 word or 1,000 word, 650 word ad. It start over. You just take what was working and change it a little bit. And appeal come at a different type of argument or a different approach that gets somebody's attention. You can do this lots of ways. You can use visual language in your first couple of lines and sort of attract the people who that's sort of how they process information. That's how they see the world. You could use auditory language. You could use visceral, kinesthetic language later. You can use logic versus emotion, questions, and dialogue. And there's so many ways that you can do this with the same core ad that you're just making minor changes to. So don't feel like when you're hearing this, like, well, I've got to be able to, you know, I went through this whole process and it was hard to write this ad and now I have to do that every three weeks? No, you spend the time to craft a wonderful ad. Really get to it. Really figure out the things you want to say and then just make little changes that swing, that have big leverage over time. That's really, really, really high value. All right, I'm going to move on, Lauren, to this next set of questions. They're the ones that I emailed to you this morning. A lot of these are super tactical and very specific, I think, to this individual. So what I'm going to do is I'm going to read each question quickly and then the ones that really need to be covered sort of in different ways, like we just need to put some resources into the academy. I'll mention that. And then anything that really pops out, Lauren, you and I can riff on just for a second. So first question in the Facebook overview part, Lauren talks about step number one, pre-qualifying. Can you tell us what the best way to assign specific events like ad the cart, purchase, et cetera, to the same pixel? Should we use Google Tag Manager? Or is there another way? How do we set the pixel so that, like for a page view, it triggers after 10 seconds? All super tactical stuff, all things that we will put in some sort of resources section in the academy because these are things that will, the how to may change over time. And it's just this, it's one of those things that it's been from a Q and A perspective. Like this isn't going to be broadly useful, but from a how do I, when I need to perspective, it will. So we'll put together a resources section for that. Also, and I don't want to sound like a jerk by saying this, I want to be very clear, I'm saying this with positive intent. A lot of these things you can Google and find out, when you go to Facebook and find out how do I, right? How do I do this, do that, right? Especially in this case, I think there's also a language, you can do this in your own native language, if that's more appropriate. We will do our best to cover all of these things and point you to resources. And I appreciate knowing things that people are thinking about, but always feel free to start with Google. This information is made available by each platform. They show you all the how to stuff. But you won't hear, and I'll just speak to you briefly, as tag manager versus a different way. In general, when you can use Google tag manager, that's the rule. Don't make tag manager an excuse for procrastination, if it scares you, if you don't want to learn how to use it. But if you're familiar enough with it to ask the question and you're okay with deploying code that way, which is a very simple way to deploy code, you can find resources that show you how to do it. I would recommend tag manager, it centralizes everything, gives you some additional capabilities. It's just worth the time. All right, next question. Suggested audience size of 500,000 to 2 million, with a slight difference as mentioned by Lauren, is it valid also for non-US markets? In my case, what about the Italian market? Do those numbers change? Lauren, what are your thoughts about audience targeting in general? Is that country specific or are those numbers that you feel comfortable with are country agnostic? I would find out what a 1% lookalike is for your country. And that is probably a safe place to mostly be, unless that is very small. If you're talking 100,000, you might want to go for more. If, for instance, Puerto Rico, is a very small country, very small. So whereas I might normally start with a 1% lookalike, then in the US would be like 2 million, in Puerto Rico that may be like 100,000, so you may want to bump it up to a 5%. I guess the long story short is that I'm comfortable with smaller audience sizes, if that's all that you can do. If you're running a local business, for instance, I mean, even if it's in the US, your audience size is gonna be limited, so do the best you can. The 500 to 2 million, kind of that range, is for specifically the US, so feel free to be okay with smaller audiences in countries that don't have the population. And we've had a client, I think it was pre-U, it was pre-U, Lauren, but we had a client where it was a very, the audience was 36,000 people total in the United States. Like that was just a very specific audience of a particular type of practitioner. And the person may, has, and continues to have a very successful business on that audience, it's a very captive audience. So it really depends a lot on what it is that you're selling. If it's broadly interesting, then your market is sort of a larger consumer market, then you can go much broader. But if it's very specific to a segment of the population, then that's the segment of the population you need to be marketing to and advertising to, that's just the reality of it. So I think I have, this person who submitted these questions, I do have additional information, Lauren, about the specifics of the business, so you and I can talk about that offline and we can maybe go back to him with some more specific thoughts on his particular situation. All right, same, let's see, same step, audience targeting. When choosing the target audience, does it make any sense to check the box, detailed targeting expansion? As per Facebook explanation, reach people beyond detailed targeting selections when it's likely to improve performance. Lauren, do you think you should check the box that says this is likely to improve performance? In the beginning, I would not, because in the beginning, what we wanna do, new pixel, new product, everything like that, in the beginning, what we wanna do is we want to train the pixel. So we wanna go to an audience that we've defined that's very tight and that's who we want seeing our ads. As the pixel on your account gets more and more data, you may be able to go to increasingly broad audiences. At that point, you may try out clicking that. I've heard of people having good results. I've also heard of people hating it. I generally don't do it. The only time I have done that is, and this may factor in to the Italian audience as well, the only time I have done that was when I was going to a local audience. So you're talking very limited. And I specified some interests, but I also clicked that button. And my logic was I'm kind of giving Facebook, in this small audience, a clear picture of, I pretty much want you going after these people, but you take the reins and drift over into other interests within this local audience if you think that'll work. And that seemed to work for that local business. So generally I would not, but there may be some cases where you can try it. And it's one of those things that, I mean, just try it out. Once you've got some pixel data, I definitely wouldn't do it until you had a pixel that had a lot of data on it. But once you've got some, try it out and see if you like the results. Great advice for everything, paid advertising related. Who knows, try something and see what happens. All right, next question. Lauren said that we can try to broaden the original audience targeting and wait for a few days to see how it goes. Are there any specific rules to know when the algorithm has already done its best, like waiting for a certain amount of days or until a certain percentage of the new audience has been covered? Lauren, any thoughts on this? Oh, it's not like a threshold where like you reach a certain point and everything clicks. I definitely think you want to give it a couple of days. I would give it probably at least three days, maybe three or four. And then just once you feel, you'll particularly have a feeling for how your other campaigns have performed. So you'll kind of have a benchmark of what you're expecting to see. And after giving it three or four days at an acceptable level of budget, so you're getting an acceptable level of impressions. If the results aren't there, relative to what you would expect from your other benchmarks, then at that point you can be like, okay, let me drop back and maybe try this again another day. We'll jump on to the next question. This is super tactical, but we have some fun with it. It's been said that we cannot use emojis to replace words in a copy. This is under top compliance mistakes. How can Facebook know if an emoji replaces or just accentuates another word? Furthermore, is it a good idea to use emojis at all? If so, how many of them should be used and which parts of the copy, beginning, middle, and et cetera? So I'll tackle this quickly just because it's, I wanna be clear about something with everyone. There is no template. There's no thing that we know works, despite what everybody tells you on Facebook about the ways that you can get 40X return on ad spend by doing this one little trick. It's all nonsense. Don't think in terms of, this is such a tactical level thinking. Don't think in these terms, right? So it's not, whether or not you use emojis in your ad copy is going to be infinitesimally impactful on what you accomplish, right? I think this was under a compliance mistake or something to draw your attention to as a, don't try to get around using like swear words or other things by trying to be funny and replace a word, right? With a whatever. Don't try to be communicative in ways that are likely to make Facebook unhappy and think you'll get away with it because you used an emoji. There is no, there's no, it will be really funny if Lauren completely contradicts me after I say this, but I am not aware and I'd be willing to bet a fair amount of money that there is no data available that there is a precise number of emojis in locations that will improve Facebook advertising performance. I can see, I can imagine scenarios where, like if you're advertising to a professional audience and you use emojis to me, that would be incredibly incongruent and would most likely depress results. You know, if I go see an ad about, you know, how to 10X my business performance and it's followed by a winking emoji, I'm out. Like I'm not paying somebody, you know, big bucks to show me how to scale my business if they're throwing emojis my way. Whereas a consumer product that's, you know, geared for a different audience, maybe, I mean, maybe that's part of the communication. Does your audience communicate with emojis? That's a big question to ask. You know, younger audience maybe, or I don't, I've never, I'm very proud of myself for never having used an emoji in my entire life. So I'm not the emoji guy, but it's just this question sort of, it's funny to me. So funny in a learning way, right? Like when we get the feedback back with some of the questions, I wonder, you know, how could we have positioned our, what we said a little bit better to make it clear that don't get caught up thinking about emojis. So having said that, Lauren, what's your take on emojis? What's the magic emoji formula? Don't give it away in case we wanna sell it as a product later. Oh, okay, well, I'll hold that back then. That's the real key. No, yeah, to your question about how does Facebook know if an emoji is replacing a word or not? Well, I mean, they have human reviewers. And if in that, human reviewers, very quick opinion that may be totally wrong, if they just think that you're trying to get around their policies, they'll reject the ad. So don't, yeah, definitely don't try to get cute with the emojis and try to replace something. Try to replace a word. In terms of using them, I mean, you know, try it out. It's a very minor thing, like Sean was saying. I've had some that, you know, it seemed to kind of work. I've heard of other people that really did well with using emojis and actually tested a copy with emojis and without emojis. And the with emojis did significantly better. So I mean, just try it out, but it is a very, very tactical case by case basis. So there is a product we could put together on emojis. Excellent. Joking, right? You're gonna have to use one then. That's true. I'd have to violate my rules. All right, let's move on to the next question. These are two questions that were directed at me specifically. So I'll answer them if you feel like jumping in. Regarding module five, part two, where I mentioned process transparency. Says, you suggest to weave into the ad copy all the actions a prospect needs to take after she clicks on the ad. What would you write in an ad redirecting to a multi-page pre-sell site setup where the prospect can access plenty of information for free but needs to give his or her email address at the end to continue the journey? This is a really, really, really good question. All questions are good. This is a great question. And it's great because I love how this person is thinking way ahead and really internalizing one of the ideas from module five and applying it broadly. It's just a really, really excellent example of that. Where this starts to seep into your thinking. So thank you for that. That makes, as a teacher, that makes me very excited. The answer, excuse me. The answer to the question really, it's because the multi-page pre-sell site provides so much value in advance and because it's pulling people forward, I am far less inclined to mention that eventually there's going to have to be an email address shared. I don't really, that doesn't, that's not really on my radar because we're not asking them to do something before they can get something. So if you think about some of the examples I used like a webinar, if you make a lot of promises in an ad about what they're going to learn, but they're not actually going to learn it or experience it or have access to it until they do a couple of things. They provide their email address, they show up, they sit for 45 minutes on a Thursday night. That to me is a little disingenuous and you will get people who, they click on the ad, they're interested in the promises that were made, but they're like, wait a minute, I'm not going through all these hoops to get that. If you compare that to a sphere of influence-inspired multi-page pre-sell site, what you're talking about in the ad, a lot of that is going to be discussed in the multiple pages of the multi-page pre-sell site. You're going to give them a lot of value in advance. Now they may, at the end of that process, three pages, four pages, one long page, however you structure that, you are going to ask of them to continue the journey for them to provide an email address. To me, that's set up by the multi-page pre-sell site. So I'm far more comfortable with, and actually I really like the idea of a multi-page pre-sell site when the first page is essentially the long form copy itself in the ad, and then it transitions to the multi-page pre-sell site for additional pages and then it gets eventually to an opt-in. I love that. I think that's just a nice sequence of events for Facebook. Lauren, any thoughts on that at all? Just from a transparency perspective? I like, for an MPPS, I really like going to the first page, just kind of having the ad built around what payoff am I going to get on that first page? Because then they immediately receive kind of that hit of, promises have been kept. I'm not teased around and I have to click around to find out what I want to find out. I have the payoff on this first page, but in the process of receiving that payoff, they're kind of hooked into clicking through the rest of the multi-page pre-sell. So that's how I personally like to handle those. Yeah, that's such a great insight. We are opening and closing loops in that first, one of the things I've said in the past, and I don't know if I, how I articulate this as much now, but it is important to open and close loops in a long form Facebook ad, but I like to open and close loops within the ad and not leave one so open and curiosity-based that somebody has to click to get the answer. But I like this idea that you just described, Lauren, of like, does the ad in page one of the multi-page, is the ad congruent with the first page of a multi-page pre-sell site so that we could look at those two things and feel like our promises were kept. However, page one also has introduced some new things to pull people forward. And in the introduction of those new things, it's congruent with the ad, but we haven't played any trickery, and we've been true to our word, we've been congruent, we have said, this is what it's about, and then that actually was what it was about, but we also started building a world for them and bringing them forward. So that's a really interesting insight that I like how you frame that. All right, let's move on to the next one. Strategic intent by Sean, that guy, you never know what he's up to. Your example number one is based on the free plus shipping offer with one upsell followed by an email sequence that leads to the core offer. You also said that it's important to build a case for the core offer early enough, otherwise we may acquire people interested only in the front end offer. Does it mean that it's okay to write a Facebook ad which talks about both the front end and the back end offer? Otherwise, which parts of the funnel ad, multi-page presale site, email sequence, must talk about the front end offer and which ones about the back end offer? Again, this is a really insightful question. There's a lot in this question. And there's a lot for me to expand upon in the original content on this idea, and I appreciate you drawing my attention to that, because to me, this is an important idea. I realized this, I don't know, years ago, but really brought it to my conscious awareness, was seeing an ad from a guy named Mike Gillette. Mike Gillette, I don't know Mike Gillette from I've been to the street, I've never met him, I have no idea anything other than sort of what I've seen. And he had a, it wasn't necessarily free plus shipping, but it's still running, you can see it on Facebook. Very successful, clearly. But the, it was weird. When I looked at the front end of the funnel, it was really strange because he has the quality and quantity of testimonials, the quality and quantity of the content, the enormity of that front end, and then you get to like, what does it cost? And it's like 10 bucks. And there was this moment like, why? Why, that's the hardest anybody's working to sell $10, to send a $10 thing I've ever seen. And it was that moment of like, well, of course, you know, complete naivety, like of course, the dumbest thing I've ever said, because he's not selling the $10 thing, he's selling the big thing. And that's why he's done so much work on the front end to sell the $10 thing, because that's just the thing to get your attention to sell you the big thing. And I don't know what the big thing is for him, I don't know what the core offer is. So that got me thinking about this idea that any good front end product always is selling the back end. That's how the game works. So your front end, like the big financial newsletter publications, their front ends, Motley Fool, Gora Financial, others, their front ends are $49 newsletters. That's their front end. You never see an offer from them that says, hey, buy a $49 financial newsletter. That's not what people are buying on the front end. What they're buying is a really big idea about some report or something that just happened or something that's about to happen or an opportunity. And you get, you're interested in the opportunity in the way that you satisfy that interest in the opportunity is that you buy this $49 front end, which then tease up all of the back ends that follow that. And they're all congruent and they're not congruent. And this is to me the central insight. They're not congruent necessarily about the product. Like the product does the same thing functionally, just more, bigger, better. They're congruent and this is what will inform your ads. They're congruent around the underlying need, the true deep desire of your audience. Now, if you're selling a financial newsletter, you're selling into either fear or greed. That's it. People are fearful that they're going to lose the money that they have or they're motivated by greed that they want to accumulate more money. Those are the two driving forces and overwhelmingly of the two greed is the primary driver in the financial newsletter market. So knowing that, when you're thinking about congruence from advertising, from front end ad to the initial offer to a core offer to other offers, what you're tapping into the thread that ties them all together is greed. And that sounds callous, right? It's not like, what do you mean? You're just, yeah, copy's all about greed. No, it's informed by the, you know that what the person, what the primary driving motivator for this person is they want to make more money. They want to accumulate money. So the tie that connects them all, so it would be incongruent to have an ad that was focused on how much money could be made. If you caught this opportunity at just the right time and you spent $50, you could have made a million dollars. And now I have the next offer that's just like that, that the next $50 to a million dollar opportunity is about to happen and here's why. And you buy the special report that tells you that and it's $49 and you also get access to this newsletter that is about how to find opportunities like this. And then the next offer you get on the back end is the world's going to collapse. You should invest in gold and protect all your money. That is completely incongruent because the front end, the ad and the front end were all based on greed and now we're based on fear. Like the court, so it's not connect, we're not connecting the dots. Instead, that same scenario would be an ad that focuses on greed, there's money to be made and there are ways people have made it and you turn $50 into a million. There's another opportunity that's coming up to turn $50 into a million and I can show you how to do that and then you buy the thing that talks about how to do that and then the core offer is how to get daily access to other ways in which you can turn $50 into millions of dollars. That's perfectly congruent. You've now connected the dots and then the next offer is the same and the next offer is the same. That's the thread that binds them. It's the emotion, the desire, the need of the audience is really what connects all the dots. Anything to contribute on here, Lauren? I'm kind of ranting on this one. This really excites me because I think this is a game changer for a lot of people in their thinking. You know, I totally agree. I think that a lot of what you're trying to do in that original ad, in your Facebook ad, you're not going to explicitly mention the core offer, but you're beginning to pay the picture about what your business does, what your product can do and as long as your front end and your core offer are kind of congruent, then you've compounded the effects so that pitching the front end offer will lead into the core offer. This is the next question. Oh, go ahead Lauren. It's worth mentioning that. Hey, Andre is on the phone. Yeah, it's me. I'm still here. Oh, what's up man? Yeah, I was just going to say that it's worth mentioning that a back end offer doesn't necessarily need to be a back end, I mean, in how you think that it's something costs one times and then the back end offer costs three, four, five, 10 times that. So the back end offer can be a front end offer, but just a different one. So which is kind of how we've built TLB. So we've got a whole bunch of front end offers, but all those front end offers are essentially back end offers for the front end. So they're all front ends and they're all back ends. And so it's just worth making that distinction. Yeah, and that's a great point. Like there's the traditional direct response front end and back end. There's also front end plus continuity, which is just more of the front end. There's so many ways to think about this. And oftentimes like certainly with TLB, TLB is a complimentary set of products that all are complimentary set of trainings that all contribute, they're sort of based on that the same foundational principles. And then they all deal with the specific challenges for those of us who are marketing our businesses, right? And building businesses in a certain way. And they all, so if you come in through one of those, at some point it's likely that your need will be for another one. But it's not a traditional, there's no $50 front end that leads to a $500 core offer. That's just, and that model is just one model. So you can imagine a product where you sell, this is very common like in a supplement space where you buy what might be say $100 for two months worth of a product, you get it for $60. And that's break even. And the idea is that if you can get somebody to take the product for 60 days, they'll see the impact on them, the positive impact. And they will want to continue when the money is to be made in continuity. It's not, you get two jars of the product for a discounted price, and then we're going to sell you a new product. It's, and that's just a different way. So lots of ways to come at this from the question on strategic intent. You just, what's important in the paid advertising side of it is what you're talking about in the ad, going to be congruent for everything in that person's experience that follows. Where I, and Lauren and I have both, we've done a project I'm thinking about right now where this was a little weird in that the front end, we've seen front ends that are so, like a free, I'm thinking of a free plus shipping front end that was so focused on the, on the front end, this free plus shipping thing that you got that it just did a very poor job of selling everything that followed. Because the free plus shipping, if you think about it, people don't want to buy a free book, right? They want what's in the book. They want what, how their life has changed by the information. So when you get really hyper focused on selling the book or the whatever the low cost thing is, it's easy to take your eye off the ball. And we're really, we are in the business of, we sell emotion. That's really what is marketers, what we do. We, not so much sell emotion, but we, emotion feeds everything that we do. Now, you've heard the phrase that we, you know, we buy with emotion and we justify with logic. I don't think that's necessarily true. People have different ways of approaching things, but it's important not to deny either of those and not to deny the fact that we are emotional creatures affected by our emotions and the emotions of people around us. And when people speak to our deep, visceral, felt emotional needs, that changes the conversation. And Facebook is a conversational, primarily emotional environment because there's no active intent. So, long and circuitous. All right, so this next question was actually from the comments in the module. But it was to me, it was interesting enough that I want to, and they're all interesting. So be aware when I say that. But this was one that I thought, it fit nicely into today's questions. So this will be the last question we have that was prepared for today, then we'll turn over to the Q and A question. So if you've been on the fence about adding your question, get your question in there, we're gonna work through them. So here, I'll read the whole thing and then Lauren, you and I can jump on this. They don't have a front-end offer like a $200 solar panel system for a camper, camper as an example, although maybe I should suggest something like that. They sell a complete system for your house at $30,000 to $40,000 per project. I'm essentially calling her leads, calling for leads from both Google ads and Facebook and relying on them to close the deal. I've relying on them being the client. I've set up a Mailchip account in late January to begin a soap opera sequence. Does that make sense? How do I measure success? Spitballing here, but you've given me so many ideas and maybe I've been in my house alone for too long, preach brother. We've all been our houses too long. All right, so I'll go through this and then Lauren, I'm gonna turn it over to you and then we'll riff a little bit. So the first paragraph I found very interesting and it speaks to what we were just talking about. So I think this is why it really jumped out at me when I was reviewing comments this morning. Client had sell solar panels. Those solar panels are $30,000 to $40,000 per installation and this question around maybe I said, I don't have a front end offer, maybe I need to sell like a solar panel system for a camper. Absolutely not. The camper, and that'll be a tiny minority of people also have a camper and the home. What you're, don't worry about a front end offer. This is a great example of the, you don't always need a front end offer conversation, right? The thing that you're selling is $30,000 to $40,000 project, photovoltaic installations at a home. I went through this process about a year ago where someone came in and I sort of heard the whole sales pitch and I had to consider this. And so I'm at least familiar with what the mentality of this is at no point would I have ever had any interest in a $200 solar panel system for a camper because A, I don't own a camper. And that's just not congruent with the need that's filled by the $30,000 to $40,000 project. So what I would ask on the front end is what is it that the market is trying to satisfy with the purchase of this installation? Because it's on the emotional side versus rational side, rationally yes over time when you amortize the cost of the installation and tax rebates in the United States and a few other factors, you do actually, it is a net positive financially. However, it takes a really long time to get that payoff. So it's not, the rational side of it is more of a justification later. Like this makes a lot of sense financially. So check that box and here's why. Okay, great. This is more of an emotional decision, right? People do this, this is a way to value signal that you care about the environment. Big, it's a big project. Everybody gets to see it. You're sort of, if the environment's your thing, it's a way also to self-satisfaction where you can feel good about moving a step forward, getting yourself away from dependence on fossil fuels. There are lots of reasons people will do this that are more emotionally based. So you don't need a transaction on the front end to get that buy-in. I would on the front end be more interested in could I get consultative calls, like set up site visits, like how could I do that on the front end? I would wanna do some sort of application though, you wanna make sure that people are talking to the right people. That's how I would handle the front end. Don't worry about having a front end offer. When you get into these questions about, I mean, others have asked these questions too, like how do I track all this stuff, especially when it's a long buying cycle? I think the simplest solution is that you need to capture in whatever you're using for a CRM where the lead came from. So, and this is actually thanks for posting that comment, Lizzie, because this was inspired by the conversation we had in the last Q and A call where you mentioned with your audience that we just have to, if we, because the question we want to be able to answer a couple of questions, is my paid advertising working? Yes or no? And how do I know if it's working? We also need to know what part of my paid advertising is working? Is it Facebook? Is it Google? Like where's the impact coming from? So to do that with a long cycle of tracking that we can't just use something like Google Analytics to say, okay, this person came in this way and then they monetized at this amount later. What we need to capture instead is when somebody becomes a lead, we need to know where they became a lead, like what got the lead? And then when that person monetizes, when they become a customer, we can then assign, it doesn't have to be, we don't have to get super sophisticated, but we just need to know that, let's just for the sake of argument, say that we know it costs $5 per lead to get leads from Facebook. And that Facebook leads, there's a certain monetization that happens. And really the two numbers that I would care about is cost per lead and value per lead. So average cost per lead and average value per lead. So those are, I don't love averages in general, this is just a really easy metric to have. And you just need to know the timeframe too. So that in an average, it costs, let's say $5 to get a lead and over whatever an appropriate time period is, the average value of those leads is whatever it is. And then you can just, then the numbers are very simple. So on average, you're paying $5 for a lead and on average leads are worth 2,500 because it's such a high volume that doesn't take a lot to make a, to have a big impact financially. So when you capture those two numbers, you really understand the business. And then I would do that for Facebook and Google separately. I don't think I would get much more sophisticated than that, sort of keep it simple at the beginning. And then you can just say, listen, for every dollar you spend on Google, you get X number of dollars in return in, within whatever, six months, 12 months, 18 months, whatever your timeframe is. Same thing with Facebook. Then you can answer the big questions. Is my paid advertising working yes or no? Is Google working for me? Yes or no? Is Facebook working yes or no? Is Facebook better or worse than Google if I have additional money to spend? Like you can answer those questions. Those are the questions that a business owner wants to be able to answer. To do that functionally, you need to capture on the front end when somebody becomes a lead, where they became a lead from. And then when that lead becomes a customer, there needs to be some indication there too. And even if you just have to do this in a spreadsheet to see that, okay, this person came in and six months later, they became a customer for this amount. And then you can start calculating these numbers. They're small numbers, unless this is a high volume business, which I sense it's not. But this will tell you, I mean, it will tell you in very accurate terms. If they spend $500 to get leads, it's just to say for the sake of our end, they spent $500 on Facebook. And a month later, they get their first conversion and it's for a $40,000 project. You now know the economics of their ad spend. Now, is it statistically significant? Who cares? They spent 500, they've got a $40,000 job. Do you know if it's repeatable? No, you have no idea. But you know the economics, you know what they spent, what they got. That's the name of the game. You just putting in place the mechanisms to do that. Lauren, any thoughts to add to this? Yeah, just tracking perspective. It is difficult to track this type of business in Facebook. You know, you're very limited in terms of your ability to attribute stuff after 28 days. I mean, you can't after 28 days. So pretty much as Sean suggested, that's the way to go. Other times too, you know, we've worked with businesses that were small enough that they might not have even had a very sophisticated CRM where they're using UTM parameters to tag people from certain ads. And it might've just been a general feel of, hey, we're comfortable spending $20 a day, $50 a day, just getting our name out there because when people purchase these, you know, high ticket services, we ask them, hey, how'd you hear about us? And they say, oh yeah, we saw you on Facebook. That's very imperfect, very inaccurate, but for a business that doesn't have the technological resources, sometimes that may be adequate. Yeah, we've run into this. I'm just thinking of a recent example too. And it's funny, I'll tell a very short story, but it's indicative of how clients sense when things are working or not working. So the client with a new marketing person who came in had worked with previous companies and worked in a certain way and expected to see certain things in a dashboard and just sort of had a way of approaching things. And one of the questions was around data, it was very clear data was not being reported into Google Analytics correctly. And we were trying to troubleshoot that, but the person who, the new marketing person was just looking at the data, so we need to stop doing all these things. And the things they wanted to stop doing, I knew I've worked with this client for a very long time, very clearly drivers of performance. So the conversation that ensued, where I threw out there, I said, here's my position. All of the data I have, all looking at everything holistically, I am confident that this ad spend is driving in excess of 10x return on ad spend. Even though the spreadsheet doesn't show it, I've come at this in different ways, I believe that's minimum what it's returning. However, if we have any doubt, let's turn it off for 30 days, see what happens. And when a client starts thinking in those terms, like, I'm not really sure if this is working, say, okay, are you willing to turn it off to find out? And they immediately viscerally are like, whoa, I don't think we should do that. Well, that on some level, you do believe it's working. So let's sort of unpack that belief. So for your own business, that's a very valid way to do things. Now, if you're buying cycles like 18 months, that's kind of miserable, that doesn't work. But when you're buying cycles, you're doing a 30 day buying cycle, you can sort of see business performance, the number of calls. I've had a couple of years ago, I had a client in the real estate space where they know amount of data, convinced them that what they were doing was working. And the only thing that finally convinced them, and it really didn't convince them, it was a non-experience, is they turned off all Google search and their phone volume plummeted, every element of their business plummeted in direct correlation with that. It was crystal clear what was happening. We had the data to know how many leads it was driving, but somebody in the office just, it was sort of one of the things like, I just don't think that really works. And we should save, and it was not a lot of money. We should just save that money. And yet, you know, turn it, and that was my point. I said, then there's a really easy solution here, turn it off, see what happens. And 30 days, there was sort of a check-in 30 days later, like, oh, what happened? And it was a fascinating conversation. Like, I don't know, it's been a really bad month. Like nothing seemed to, phones not ringing, whatever. I'm like, do you see, like, say it out loud? Do you see what's happening here? But sometimes clients don't. And this is not a client call specifically, but just be aware that you, if you were working with clients, you will run into this issue. If you're not, and it's your business, you kind of know, right? Like, you have a sense, like, oh, you know, I started spending more money last month, and nothing really seemed to change. Well, guess what? You don't need a dashboard to tell you that what you were spending the money on, either one of two things was happening. Either everything that was successful before started to fail, and the new thing that you did took off, which would be a pretty unlikely event, or the thing that you added really hasn't had a meaningful impact on your business. Trust that judgment, right? Just be willing to go, okay, I sort of, I kind of, I sense what's going on here. It's not always about the dashboard. It's about what the dashboard gets you to think about. All right, that's all the questions that were prepared in advance. I've got something to add. I'm just gonna think out loud here for a moment. So, you know, you could give a coupon that's obviously trackable. I understand that obviously you've got better, the ads have been shown to everybody, so you can't display a coupon in an ad that's unique to that person, therefore you can tie it back in some way. I don't know the answer to this, but I'm just thinking, again, I'm just thinking out loud, looking at various dynamic parameters that are available to you within Facebook. Ad ID, Ad Set ID, Campaign ID, there's all these weird IDs, and again, I don't know if this is even possible, but some way where you can pass through some unique IDs using Ad ID, Ad Set ID, that you can tie back, and then when they click on it and it goes to a landing page, it just passes that number out onto the screen, which is obviously unique to them. They then scribble that down because it's written as a coupon code valid for one year or whatever, but then they get to keep that little coupon that then identifies them downstream somewhere. Again, I don't know if this is possible, but... Yeah, I mean, there are things that do that. That's an interesting idea, right? And it's interesting in the idea itself, sort of functionally, but also conceptually. What could you do for somebody at a point of first contact that gives them a token, like an artifact, a physical thing or a reminder thing that is beneficial to them and beneficial to you? I mean, traditionally, this is a coupon code, this is all kinds of different things, and technology has automated that in many ways. I think there's also an opportunity to... How could we make it more meaningful? How could we, and especially, a $30, $40,000 project like this, what could you get that would serve the business's interest but also be useful to the customer? And that's just something to think about. You know, oftentimes, in the site visit, you're brought some collateral or whatever, but it would be interesting to have a... Something that connected all the dots that told you things like aging, how long did it take for someone to become a customer? You could really rely on, you could go back and actually see, like, this person became a lead on this day, and it's 18 months later, they became a customer. And then when we look at this data, that seems to be the norm. Like, that's a fascinating thought. All right, let's... There's a help page on Facebook for Business saying, upload unique promo codes and bar codes to your offer ad. So it seems like there's native support for some mechanism where it can rotate through... I mean, through codes. And it will uniquely display it to somebody. Yeah, I mean, there is that functionality for offline tracking. It just doesn't... What is it, 28 days, Lauren? Is that... It's not... Yeah, it's 28 days to be attributed to the ad. Right. And there are tools that do this too. Third-party tools, Wicked Reports has a way that they approach this very similar. Like, just knowing, like, what character... Bundle of characteristics is somebody when they become a lead and then what do they do over time? And then when they purchase in what quantities and it goes back, I mean, the beauty of Wicked Reports is that it's always going back in assigning revenue to things that happened in the past based on what happens over time. It's just a foundationally interesting idea that if somebody became a lead six months ago and over the course of those six months, they've purchased eight or nine times from other, maybe other ads or emails or other things, that data is still fed back into the original lead acquisition to let you know, like, sure, it cost you this much to acquire this customer, but now six months in, this customer has generated this... Like, the economics of that ad from six months ago are fundamentally different. It's interesting. This is a really interesting thing to think about from a metrics perspective is, and I'm not suggesting you out and buy a tool. I'm just saying that it's the thinking behind the tools that's really fascinating. Like, how would you, if you owned the business, what questions would you ask and then how would you answer those questions? And that starts, and then that changes your thinking about how you do things, you know, what motivates behavior in little things. Like on the front end to Andre's point, we gave somebody a code and we know that an inflection point where people are really, like, maybe we know that most conversions happen within five months, but that others convert, you know, that, or maybe that would say there's an inflection point between four and six months is when a lot of the magic happens, that lots, you know, the 80% or so of the conversions happen in that window. Well, knowing that and knowing that they had a code in the beginning, that creates this opportunity to send them back the code and have a sequence of things to, and then to assign to that code some sort of financial benefit or some sort of extra benefit. Say that, you know, when you first signed up, you got this code as well, remember it's still active and not only that, you now, that code will get you the following three or four benefits should you choose to buy this, you know, this installation in the next whatever, three days or whatever. It doesn't have to be time bound, but that then you draw their attention and you're restarting the conversation, but you're doing it around this thing that's unique to them. And that thing then takes on some value. And one of the things we know, you know, and Andre and I have been talking a lot about a book that I just finished recently, the Undoing Project about Danny Connman and Amos Tversky, but one of part of their research and behavioral economics and the psychology behind it is that we are, we have a lot greater aversion to loss than we do to gain. So if you say click here to get this thing that will give you, you know, these three benefits, we treat that psychologically very differently than saying, hey, you got this code six months ago and this code has the following value. You already have this, you get X off, you get this in your installation, you get whatever. And that you will lose that at some point in the future, you know, that code expires, we just wanna let you know that code expires in 90 days. Well, now you're about to lose something that's valuable and that changes, I'm not suggesting we trick people in any way, this is just how our brains think. So it's a way to understand how we can use our advertising and our tracking to motivate the behavior, you know, in good ways and positive ways to motivate the behavior that we ultimately want that's beneficial for us and beneficial for the right people. We're not going to trick somebody into buying $40,000 solar installation because we used insights from behavioral economics, but we're more likely to move along to say all that was going to happen anyway. So that's the idea behind this. All right, looks like we have seven questions so far. So first question, what are your thoughts and experiences on long form ads for pre-qualifying for local businesses, specifically for a healthcare clinic that sells IV nutritional therapy as in IV hangover bags and IV vitamins. I'll start Lauren, and then actually you go first Lauren, and I'll jump in. I'll have you disagree with me if I say something stupid. No, I think pre-qualifying long form ads work great with local businesses, particularly because what you're going to be doing is with a local business, you're only targeting a local region, your audience is much smaller, which means you're going to have to go much more broad in your targeting. So pre-qualifying is, you know, you can make an argument it's even more important when you have that type of a broad local audience. So absolutely, it sounds like your product would be one that would benefit from pre-qualifying and some long form explanation, maybe answering objections, all of the benefits, the exact process, what does it all entail? I think that sounds like a good strategy for your particular business. And this is, I'm just took a little extra time to think about this one because I actually, I do, I have 150 grams of IV vitamin C every month, I've done that for years. So I'm sort of, I understand this particular example that you're giving. So knowing before I knew that was available in my area, that was to me was search based on some health issues. So I don't have the same experience with sort of seeing it in Facebook, but it certainly would, that certainly would have piqued my curiosity. I think the hangover back idea, that's a little bit different because that's a, that's really something that you want to let people know, I'm thinking of an area in your bio, which is a big college town, like, that's something that you want people to be aware of before they need it. Because they're unlikely when they're, when they're in the moment of needing it, it's unlikely that they're going to be in the best frame of mind to be scrolling through Facebook and hope they stumble on it. So that would be something where you would build up this, you would let people know in advance that this thing exists in their community. But it is a good example of where, you know, pre-qualifying, I think for this particular thing to let people know, things like the person who is doing the IV is trained, you know, either they're full of automists or they're, maybe you have everybody, they're nurses, whatever, whatever the deal is, like answer the potential objections in there. Because this, in particular, people don't like it needles jammed into their veins. It's just not something we love as a species. So even though the benefits are extreme, you need to be very clear that, you know, they're going to go and sit down and have something put in one of their veins that the person who's doing is professional and all care is taken, whatever. So there is some pre-qualifying, like my wife is terrified of needles. She's not going to do this. You're pre-qualifying for her. If you've diminished the IV part and focused on the nutritional therapy and got her really excited about the benefits of nutritional therapy, and then she showed up for either a consultative call or in person and found out there was an IV, you would have wasted your money. She would have found that out on the front and she would have asked the question. But you're not just, you're not trying to get people excited about the benefit of the therapy first and then tell them later, by the way, we're going to stick a needle in your arm. You got to deal with that right up front. There's a significant percentage of the population that that is a non-starter. They're not, they're not going to do it because of that. So don't try to convince them to. Now there are people on the fence too. So you could in the, and don't just assume you can't change anybody's mind. In my experience, I've probably had in excess of 25, maybe closer to 30 different IV treatments, vitamin-focused IV treatments. So I do know that it doesn't really hurt, very rare that it hurts. So you could include some of that, like common objections and these are the fears. But there are also people who have very different experiences, small veins, veins that collapse, you know, like who knows. So this is an example where I would just be right up front with this is, these are the benefits, but this is what you need to do to get the benefits. And don't try to diminish that because you're just wasting your time and their time in this particular audience. So and in general terms, and pre-qualifying for local businesses, yes. I mean, what is unique to your business? Do people have to sit up, does there a long drive? You know, is it not in an urban area? They've got to drive an hour and a half outside of town, but it's worth it because this incredible selection or the experience, tell them that. Some people aren't going to drive 90 minutes, some people don't have a car. Like just be up front about that, where you are, what you do, like answer those questions as much as possible. Don't dwell on them, but answer them. All right, done. Next couple of questions. How do you start with these, Lauren? I'm very curious about this, to hear your take on this. So hello, Lauren and Sean. How to promote network marketing funnels and compliance with Facebook policy? Lauren. Oh man, you know, I mean, I think the technical answer is you can't. The ads, it is against policy to advertise multi-level marketing networks. Facebook might not be the best, the best medium. Yeah, and that's my answer, there are two answers to this question. One, one straightforward and one a little more nuanced. Straightforward answer, how do you promote a product on, how do you promote a product on Facebook that Facebook says you can't promote? The answer is in the question, you can't. And you shouldn't try. That's the real answer here. Don't try to break Facebook's policies. If I can't stress this enough to anyone, if you are in a business vertical that Facebook doesn't like, and you're trying to trick Facebook into liking it, you are setting yourself up for heartache and headache and misery and pain, period. You are pushing a rock uphill for the rest of your professional life. That's the short answer, that's the answer I hope you internalize. You cannot trick the platform. Those days are over. And Facebook in particular is tied to you as an individual. So that creates all kinds of excitement. The, that's, now there are three, if you give me 10 minutes I'll have like 20 answers here. So there's, there's three, I'm up to three answers now. That's answer number one, that's the answer. Answer number two is, use YouTube or the GDN, right? I mean, just go someplace else where there, it's less problematic. The third answer is yes. There are scenarios, this is the nuanced answer, there are scenarios where you can promote something on Facebook that gets people into your world. And that, that world, the front end of that world, the world that's visible to Facebook does not promote network marketing. But later through email, other touch points, you can already do it, you then lead somebody to that door. In the past, that was okay. Right in the past, Facebook had this interesting phrase that Facebook used to care about what happened between the ad and the landing page. And they said that anything after the landing page in their words was of the user's volition. Meaning that was a choice the user made at the landing page and it was not Facebook's concern. Facebook is deep, deep in the funnel now. They're much more like Google and that they're, they're looking at entire domains, entire processes, the funnels. So it's going to get more and more difficult to push out the thing that you're really doing. I mean, I know someone who, I don't know if this is still true, but very recently was selling gun lasers on Facebook. Like how do you sell gun lasers on Facebook? You can't, right? But it was exactly that. There was a front end, sort of front end lead acquisition that later turned into lots of things sold by email. So you can do that.
