And if this is one of many things that you're doing and you can, in six months of this working or six days is worth it. Okay, figure out a way to bring somebody into your world for the underlying need that they're trying to satisfy. And then later you reveal, not it reveals the wrong word, but you eventually lead them to the place that how they do that. So you're, the Facebook ad, the lead acquisition is about the why. It's about the emotion. You're bringing them into your world. And then later you're showing the mechanism, which is network marketing. That's the nuanced answer. I prefer the first answer though, which is just don't try to do it. It's just you're a target that you're not gonna be happy. Second, our next question, same person, similar vein. Lauren, to start with this, I'm not as familiar with dropshipping. So the question is, do you know how to prevent my accounts from having problems when I promote dropshipping products? Lauren, what's the deal with dropshipping on Facebook? Dropshipping is compliant, but Facebook doesn't seem to like it. And Facebook has a lot of the, a lot of suspicion around dropshipping. So it is a difficult industry to be in. What you can do is Facebook likes to see brands that have, that are good citizens of Facebook, that have positive interactions. You've got a page that has lots of organic content on it. You function like a real business would, like a real brick and mortar business would. They like to see that positivity and they also like to see good reviews. So one of the things that Facebook does is they will follow up with people who have purchased products off of your Facebook ads. They'll follow up with them and they'll ask, are you pleased? You know, please rate this advertiser. And if you get a lot of negative feedback, that is bad. And you can get pages, accounts, all sorts of stuff banned. There was kind of a rash of a lot of problems around the time that the coronavirus hit originally in China, because what happened was a lot of dropshippers who were using Chinese fulfillment centers, those fulfillment centers were shut down. They couldn't mail out and people were seeing significant delays on the products that they purchased. So they gave significant negative reviews. So lots of dropshippers had problems. But the basic gist of your question is, how to prevent your accounts from having problems? Just act like a real business, not like a fly by night kind of come in and let me just put up a Facebook ad and send it off to some Chinese fulfillment center. Have a brand interact with people, post organic content. Make sure that your customers are really, really, really happy. That's the best way to approach it. Yeah, I'll add one thing to that, which I hope is a deeper resource to consider. There is a podcast, Tim Ferriss did a podcast. I don't have the link or I drop it in, but it was with two young men. One was Dartmouth. I don't think he even finished his degree. I can't remember where his partner was from. But two young college kids, actually I think they were both from Dartmouth. Two young college guys, Matt realized they had some interest in common, built a, I think a multi-million dollar business, it's like a chair cushion or something. Go find that podcast and listen to it because they weren't really, they're not drop shippers, but they did the thing that is kind of common now, you see it's happening because of fulfillment by Amazon and some other things, but what sort of the model that's out there now that drop shipping is kind of a corollary to is you go find a product on Facebook or I'm sorry, you go find a product on Amazon that's doing really well, that's got a lot of sales volume, and then you have someone in China make a product that's the same except a little bit better based on what there are some comments in the reviews. So you just make something that it's already selling well, but you make it just better enough and different enough that it's a new product. That's what these guys did. And of course, over time, this is a race to the bottom. I mean, it's probably one of the downsides of the sort of the systemic effects of fulfillment by Amazon is creating this environment that enables these, some things that we probably don't want as a society. Certainly don't want a business, this is kind of a challenge, but drop shippers, drop shipping is a variant of that. You're just saying, I don't make the thing, I don't even know, I just, I advertise the thing, but there's a lot to be learned from their story. So if you're willing to accept some homework, and this is anyone who's interested in the FBA or drop shipping or other things, you're just interested in a good business story. Go listen to that podcast interview. Tim Ferriss, couple of guys, Dartmouth grads, back, I think if they're back pain, and it's like a chair cushion is the thing. But it was fascinating. It's, I assigned it to my entrepreneurship class. And many of them are interested in FBA fulfillment by Amazon there. So it's a really good look at how that works. You just have to ask if that's the business you want to be in. Do you want to be in the advertising somebody else's product business, or do you want to be in the continually trying to stay ahead of everybody else who's trying to make your product better too? I mean, it's a tough world to be in. They've been very successful. That business I have no interest in, but that doesn't mean it's a great business. It's not a great business. I mean, it just means I don't have interest in it. Some people are fascinated by that. And it's a great way to go add value to the world. So, you know, that's not the direct answer to the specific question that you're answering. But I think that will give you a lot of context and nuance well beyond the answer. And of course I echo what Lauren said as well. The one thing I would add, I'm debating whether or not to add this, but I want to add this and I want you to know that I'm telling you this with positive intent, not to be dismissive, but both of these questions have something in common, which is you're putting yourself in a position to have challenges with the business, with advertising the business. Don't overlook that, right? Ask yourself, why not make it easier for yourself? How could you make business easier for yourself? If it's hard to advertise what you're selling, there's a reason for that. And it's not going to change. It's not going to get easier to do MLM stuff with paid advertising. It's that is a business model that Facebook and Google and others are desperately trying to eradicate from their platforms. So, what is the opportunity cost to you of the time that you're investing, pursuing business models that are less likely to produce the positive result that you want? If you took that same available time and applied it in business models that are far easier, I suspect you would get a much greater result with far less frustration. So, if you and I were sitting next to each other, having a beer or an iced tea, whatever your deal is, and we were having this conversation as friends, after I answered your technical questions, what I would then say to you, friend to friend, is maybe make this a little easier on yourself. Let's go find something fun to do that is not as hard. We're parking a downhill slope versus trying to push a rock uphill. So, I say that as, although we're not formal friends, but I say that as a friend. All right, next question. Hi, Lizzie. I'm guessing it's a retargeting audience that's really, really, really small. And, you know, in that case, if after two days, it's only been shown to 23 people and you're already at a frequency of two, it's probably just not going to be a long-term, successfully good audience to go for. If it is retargeting, it may be like a time-based thing. And in that case, frequency of two might be, you know, okay, because maybe I'm seeing the ad over the course of three days, but then I'm moving out of that and I'm no longer part of that retargeting audience. So in that case, you know, ad fatigue isn't as big of a deal because you're constantly cycling through audiences. Also, sometimes frequency of two, as long as it's working, it's working. So if you are doing, if you had a webinar funnel, for instance, and people who watch the webinar, you're retargeting them to a high ticket program, a frequency of over two, three, four, five, you may be able to hit that and still have a positive return on ad spend and still not have a lot of negative feedback on the ads, just because it's such a high ticket item to a relatively small audience that it's worth it. So obviously the more people packed into your local, your local metropolis there, then you could still put on some interest targeting and have a decent size audience. All right, this is a question for Andre, who I assume is still on the call. Andre, can you please elaborate further on what you said about how you view your various products in terms of backend and front end. It sounded very helpful, but please elaborate. Would you do it differently if you had a $97 product? Most of your products cost the same. I currently only have one product at $497. I am considering adding a cheaper, say $97 for a front end offer. Take it away. Well, all of ours are essentially the same price, so $500, $495, and there's nothing before that. Now, the way that we've chosen to rig our system is, you need to us maybe, but conceptually for us, our front end product is free. So it's all of our pre-sell pages, all the free content that we work really hard to make compelling and amazing. And that is our front end. And our backend essentially is all of our courses that happen to cost $500. For us, it would make no sense at all to stick something or engineer it in a way that we now are gonna have something for $97 just so there's a stepping stone because that would devalue all the free stuff that is essentially our front end. So I think traditionally, this is how things have been taught, if you're selling something for $500, well, maybe you can sell something for $97 as a stepping stone or even $10. Well, you can't sell something for $10 if you do an amazing job of putting free stuff out there. If you've got no free stuff, whereas it's essentially just a whole bunch of opt-in pages everywhere and some articles, well, then maybe you can sell something for $10. So again, it just depends on how you want to design the experience for the prospects and customers. There's a customer journey for us and all of our products are front ends, they're all back ends because they're all slot into a different part of the customer journey. There's no real overlaps, they all eventually solve one big problem, which is the system of business. So again, there's no right or wrong way. That's not to say that anybody that does have a $10 that then goes to something higher or $97 that goes to something higher, that's, I'm not suggesting that is incorrect. It's just a different way of doing it. I don't know, Sean, do you have anything else to add? Yeah, I dropped a link in the chat to the fourth email in the traffic engine series where we talked about pricing and how we both think about pricing. And I think there's a lot of nuance in that that contribute to this question. And as we, Andre, hasn't had a front end offer, but what I think something worth drawing attention to though is conceptually there is a front end in sort of an Andre's world. And that front end is a requirement of attention. So instead of paying with currency, like here's 10 bucks can be the thing. If you've ever tried to sign up to become a lead in tiny little businesses, it's hard, right? It's work, you've got to read, you've got to engage. That's a front end. It's just, it's not a front end people pay for with dollars. They pay for it with time. So it's important to think about what are you asking of your audience? Because traditional direct response, certainly scaled straight to offer business models, try to grease the wheels. They try to make the process from awareness to customer as slick and as fast. And when I say slick, I mean frictionless, not slick like design, but frictionless to get somebody to go from ad to sales copy to purchase as quickly as possible. So you lower the price point, you load on the bonuses, you do all of these things, you turn to dial it because what you're trying to do, the conceptual framework here is that you're trying to make it easier for somebody to become a customer. Well, the only difference between that and saying, you can come into my world, but the price of entry is that you need your worldview changed and you need to internalize and accept these ideas and really know what it's all about here. That's a front end. It's just paid for with a currency of time and attention versus a currency of dollars. So I think Andre has had it probably one of the greatest front ends any businesses had at all time. You just can't, you pay for it differently. And that's what I think that creates systemic emergent effects to which we're talking about in a different email series. It's going on for sphere of influence and auto responder madness now. So it's fresh on my mind, but that's an emergent effect. It is a, you get a very different kind of prospect when you say you can be part of my tribe for 10 bucks or you can be part of my tribe by spending an hour or two consuming and thinking about this content. It doesn't, we don't have to spend a lot of time thinking about the differences in those two prospects, right? I can spend 10 bucks and I can get the thing that says, now I've done the thing, I can check the box and never have to open the thing. I scratch the itch off, I go, that's a very different experience and is likely to produce a very different type of customer than saying before you can even become a lead, you need to internalize some of these ideas. This is what we're about here. And if this sounds good to you, keep giving us your attention for a while. And at some point you will have an opportunity to be part of the list, the tribe, but not until you've consumed all this content, not at your head up and down a lot. That's a really different front end. It produces a very different overall effect. Yeah, Sean mentioned something worth highlighting there that you or people can, they'll sell a lower front end product just so they can generate a customer. And then they think that the customer suddenly becomes magical and you can sell other stuff to it, that's fair enough, that is true to an extent. But it's also possible to make customers selling them nothing. So, we make lots of customers before they become our customer. So, our customers start off as prospects, but in their minds, they customers already, they just haven't had the opportunity to make a purchase yet. That just comes downstream, maybe the doors are closed, but don't think that a customer becomes a customer when they make that transaction with you. It can happen way before that. Such a profound insight. Like I'm sitting here just immersed in that answer, because what an amazing idea of making, of thinking about people as customers who have not spent money yet, which I mean, obviously if nobody ever spends money, that's not the right business. But if you make, if people spend money eventually and you treat them as customers because of their investment of attention, again, everything downstream changes, everything changes. That's not really the topic of the call, but that's, yeah, we need to continue that conversation, because that's fascinating. All right, let's stay on track. I wanna riff on that more, but let's stay on track. We have two more, I don't know, there are more questions. Yeah, there are more questions. All right, next couple of questions, few questions in one. When we are setting up our first campaign, do we need to exclude Google partner sites? Yes, do that, exclude those in general. You can add them back later, just compare, but you're most likely gonna see a depression performance. So in general, Google ads turn off Google partner sites. If I'm running an ad for a local business, do I need to specify that the language is English? It's for a local business and people who don't speak English, locally might still need the service. I would specify in English. I mean, it's, I don't know, that might have some, I mean, if you're in a bilingual area, then do your ad, have separate ads for English and the other language. But if the ads written in English, if the nuance of the language is important, I would still specify English, just tiny little, I mean, it may make no, no, it may create no benefit, but it may eliminate just a tiny little bit of drag. So yes, you said to try to get 10 clicks a day, most of the local keywords have less than 200 clicks a day. I'm not sure I understand what the question was that. So, I'll just explain what 10 clicks a day is. The reason I want, there are a few reasons that I like, for Google ads, I like this metric of 10 clicks a day, plus or minus, because it does a couple of things. One, it keeps you out of the one click a day territory. Right, like this is a perfect search term. And I get, you know, every week, I get somewhere around five to eight clicks, but you can optimize that. Like you might have those later, they might be worth it, but you're not going to learn how to do Google ads that way. Like the way to learn, and that's what this program really is about, is to get a result and to learn and to have those two things working in tandem with each other. You're learning by getting results and optimizing results and iterating your way to success with a core set of skills. That's the goal for this course. So 10 clicks a day is a sweet spot where you're not going to spend so much money that hopefully, there are some markets where you can spend a fortune at 10 clicks a day, but you're not going to spend so much money, you're going to put yourself out of business, you're going to get data in a reasonable period of time that you can act on, and you're going to get a result. Now, I also use that because it concentrates ad spend. What I've seen happen, this is probably one of the among the most common mistakes I've seen people make, is they have X amount of dollars to spend, and let's just assume you have $500 a month to spend, and you could put all of that 500 into a single keyword phrase and get a result to know if it was profitable in four or five days, or you could spread that money out across five or six keyword phrases, and you wouldn't get a result for all of those until days 25 or 30. Overwhelmingly, people spread their ad spend out across keyword phrases, and hoping whether it's specifically like one ad group, and then one keyword or one keyword phrase, or just sort of conceptually, they put five or six phrases into an ad and off they go, same result. And what happens is you dilute your results, and you don't know what's working for a really long period of time, and most of the time things don't work. So you're burning cash at an accelerated rate to find out that 80% of what you were burning cash on was not going to be positive, but you didn't find that out for three weeks. What I like to do is say, focus all of my ad spend on one thing to get a result as fast as possible to know if that thing stays or goes, and it's sort of binary, it's red, yellow, green. Red, it didn't perform. I've spent whatever, I've got 75, 80, 90 clicks, 100 clicks, nothing, there's no indicator that's going to work, I turn it off, it's red. Green, I spend money, I make money. Excellent, I consistently spend money, make money. Perfect, that stays, that's making me money, so it's not a cost anymore, I don't have to think about it in terms of ad budget. And then yellow is, this isn't really working great, but I think it has potential, I could do some work and make it better, okay, well then spend your time there. But if you have a couple of reds and one green and a few yellows, and it just, you extend the amount of time it takes to figure that out, and then you shorten, or that you make it take forever to optimize, it's just all of that is not in your best interest. So that 10 clicks a day metric is really supposed to be the number that will get you enough results daily that you can optimize with some reasonable frequency without burning cash unnecessarily. To a long answer to a short question. Hey Lauren, you wanna chime in on me the Google ads questions, that'd be kind of fun. Lauren doesn't do any Google ads stuff, it would be fun to jump in there. I'm gonna stay in my lane there. All right, next question, these are all agency questions, and I think I've actually answered these in, I think I've answered this already in the content for the agency upgrade. So how would you guys start an agency from scratch? What would be your first step? You would make to create a very organized business and closing the first client. I'm actually working in the company's processes, I want to have clients in the US, but I don't have clear the niche I wanna work with, but I know I want people that work with professional services, like psychology, that. All right, so overall this content is kind of like the, how do I approach agency stuff as covered in client services, I just wanna sort of put all of that in that thing. If you weren't, for some reason, aware of that, when you just have access to the core things, you weren't aware of that, reach out by email, we'll try to figure that out. If you have access to it, please go read it, as I cover a lot of this ground. The thing I think is broadly useful in as an answer to this question is, in my experience, the way to do the agency side of things, which is also a way to do the business in general, well is to figure out what you do really, really well and put that adjacent to people who value what you do really well more than others. That's the magic formula. So because that increases the frequency of those interactions, you get all these compounding effects. So when you do something exceptionally well, there are lots of people who will benefit from that. That's just the name of the game. I do paid traffic pretty well, lots of people could benefit from that, but within that group of people who can benefit from it, there are a much smaller subset who overvalue the things that I do well. And those are the people that I want to work with. They're more fun to work with, they pay more because it's worth more to them. I can create far more value. Everything is better about it. So that's the framework I would use. It's a framework I think about in business in general. You can think about this framework for writing ad copy. The framework permeates everything. What few things do you do exceptionally well and to whom are those few things exceptionally valuable? They overvalue your expertise. Go find those people and build an incredible business from those people. Don't try to find the people who could benefit, but don't overvalue. That's a recipe for frustration. Ask me how I know. Yeah, and that idea of, which I think that was my first experience with sphere of influence. Like that was the thing that really just lit me up with it was it's that moment when somebody's world completely changes because you've shown them, you've given them a lens. I'll know the metaphors, red pill versus blue pill from the matrix. What pick your metaphor, rose-colored glass is anything. But what the insight is, I think a really good multi-page presale site takes somebody from the world that they currently occupy to a world that they hope exists, they want to exist. But the time in between is showing them that that world has always been there for them to see. They just haven't known how to look for it. And you've shift their perspective. And once that perspective shifts, it's like, then they can't not see it. They're like, well, of course. Oops. Do we lose somebody? Lost sound. Yeah, I think we lost Sean. Well, well, he's getting on. I'll go ahead and answer. No, to answer the question, yes, the Facebook compliance, the issue is that you're not allowed to call out personal characteristics of someone. So you can't imply that they're a certain race, a certain age, any defining personal characteristics. Now that's the policy, but it kind of morphs over time to now they're saying, don't say anything about, imply anything about the person reading the ad. And then that has come to where we're hearing now, don't even say the word you, don't even say the word you're. Now, I used to write ads and I mean, there was no problem saying something like, let me tell you about this, because I'm not making any claim about that person's characteristics. I'm just referencing them as the reader of the ad, right? Cause they're reading the ad. That's fine, that's technically compliant. We're just being told by reps, don't even say you, don't even say you're. That's for the ad. Now for the landing page, the same rules don't necessarily apply for the you and you're. I think you can push it a little more and maybe have some more personal characteristics that are mentioned, because they've taken an action to click over to that landing page. So in a sense, they've raised their hand somehow. So you may be able to reference personal characteristics a little more. That being said, sometimes Facebook is just, they go by gut feel, they go by, it's not necessarily what's cut and dry, it's not necessarily what makes sense, what's logical, what's reasonable. It is a human Facebook reviewer somewhere who quite frankly is a low level employee. This is probably their first job. Maybe they're hungover, they got in a fight with a girlfriend the night before, and they're gonna make a snap judgment on the landing page. So it's not always gonna be reasonable or consistent or rational. You're probably all right. You'd be all right using you and your, just as Sean was saying, don't really come hard at it and be a little wary of personal characteristics calling out sensitive characteristics, particularly if they're, if it's like a fat loss product, calling someone out for being overweight or some medical type of product or race, gender, that type of stuff, Facebook might be a little leery about. Sure, it says, online programs question, urgency, next to me, okay, yes. Online programs question, urgency versus available when people need it most. Always available to buy versus strict open closed dates for enrollment dates, like SOA, SOI slash ARM seem to be now. What are your thoughts on this? That's more of a Andre and you question, Sean. Yeah, Andre, want to jump on that for a second? Yeah. I think it's, I mean, they clearly both, both, both models work. I mean, each one comes with its own pros and cons and constraints and issues. I think it just depends on what sort of business in a way that you want. You know, Seth Godin does the open closed thing, like hard open and close for all of his stuff. And I think it works really well because then he doesn't have to use fake scarcity and at the same time, as he knows that scarcity and urgency is a is a trait that he wants to use, but he wants to use it in a way that's he, that's congruent with with, you know, how he thinks and what he teaches. So I guess he's chosen to do the hard open and close and that works perfectly fine. He's got a team that can, that can help him execute on that. Other people just want to have that evergreen thing. So you need to engineer some level of scarcity in different ways. They certainly know right or wrong way. Yeah, I think the way I would think about this. Who, how does how does that you answering that question affect your audience? Is there a reason is they get a much greater benefit if it's if there is a time bound closed system or if it's if it's open enrollment. So I just enrolled in something 48 hours ago that I wanted, I want it is a it's a group environment at any given point there are 50 or 60 people in sort of a quasi similar within one or two weeks of each other cohort. I'm not in that. I'm not, I'm not there. This is something that for me as the user, I'm I'm going to get benefit from that because I can start and go through all eight weeks at my pace the way I want to. Not and there really is an enormous benefit to me. And I think there would be there might be some distraction to me if I had to wait to do that with a group of people. Now, I've done a very similar same topic, same time frame, same everything, same people version of this in the past where it was a cohort that started and ended in a specific time and that annoyed the hell out of me because I had to wait three weeks to start and by the time it's like the three weeks got there, I was I was mad that I had to wait. I like so like is the benefit to you as the provider is the benefit to the audience. Right now, within the thing that I just signed up for that, there might be a code there might be some significant percentage of people in that that would just really want to have more of that sort of that that group vibe. So I don't have in this maybe not a definitive answer. But I think asking like we tend to come at this question from our perspective as the creators, the business owner, you know, what makes the most sense? How do I want to do this? What will maximize revenue? What's like, well, good, ask those questions, but start with a question. Is there anything about what I'm sharing, teaching, selling, providing that is more beneficial, significantly more beneficial when it is delivered in a time bound format that begins and ends, opens and closes? Or is there a benefit to an open ended or some combination? And then you could also give people a choice. You know, you could for both and right. I live in a both and world, not either or right. Do if you have something that you could say about a solid third of the people want to do this beginning to end by themselves. And the most want to do it as part of a group. And there's this interaction like great. Give somebody a choice on the front end. You can start this course today and you will have your experience will be X, Y and Z. If you're more interested in the content, you're an individual solo learner, you want to go step by step through it and you are not as interested in the group work, then this is a perfect solution. However, if you get a lot of benefit working through these things with others and you want to be part of a larger group, our next group starts on X state. Excellent. Now the audience, everybody can be happy. Now, can you just build that in a day? No, but you can do both. There's no reason you can't do both. So I would say both and is an effective approach. I'm a marketing product that's subscription based that helps get them new clients. What approach would you take to advertising on Facebook? I'm going to have Lauren answer this in a second. But Facebook should be advertising this group for you already on your behalf. 9000 members, if it's active, there's a lot of engagement. Facebook should be promoting your group. So I would let Facebook promote my group. Lauren, and I'm sure there are ways to augment that. So Lauren, how would you what do you think on this one? Yeah, well, it looks like your goal is to sell the product and not necessarily to get people into the group. So what I would do is post organically, you know, make sure you've got your your funnel set up, all your pixels firing, everything else like that. Go ahead and post organically into your group several times and hopefully get some sales that way. That way, the pixel data can begin to be accumulated that way. And then you just advertise like you'd advertise for any other hair salon owner type of product having that group. Although that's a great asset to help you get sales. It might not necessarily be any use, any tremendous use in helping you with targeting or helping you run ads. The only real benefit it could be was that those people may kind of help give you your pixel data. But but you you pretty much handle it the same way that you'd handle any other ads. Yeah, I love this business model, by the way, Andrew. I think there's a lot of potential here and nine thousand members. Is that's huge. So it's a huge group and it's excellent. So I think your opportunity is in the group itself that you if you're if you're active in there and you're really and I'm sure you are just saying I was thinking out loud, if you're active in there, you're contributing value. You're really showing people how to get the things that matter most of them. Give a lot there. And then it's some frequency that's not contrived, but it's some frequency. Make sure that they know that there's more that they can get from you outside of the group. And then what that looks like and what the entry point is and that can be. You know, it can be anything. A couple thousand dollars. It can be a thousand dollars a month. I mean, you know, I don't it's really is it's what's the value to your audience. And what value can you create for them? And the price point that makes sense with all of those variables and feels good to you and feels right to them. So and you'd want to do higher volume, lower costs, higher costs, lower volume, like answer all those questions. But that you've built a very valuable asset that you can monetize as is. And I would be less far less concerned about getting more people into the group than I would be about monetizing the attention of the that you already have. I would I would really figure that out and I would really engage and find out what those 9000 members want. And I would spend the next years giving it to them and every conceivable way that creates value for them and for you. I would be far less concerned about adding more. All right, next question. Does a long form Facebook ad connect to direct e-commerce page for a low value offer rather than multi-page pre-sell site? Is long form Facebook ad multi-page pre-sell site? Is that more suitable for medium to high value offers? So there's no clear answer to this. The couple of the key things that pop out for me. E-commerce page and low value offer. I don't I'm not sure what that means. Right. So I don't know what a low value offer is. If that's like an unexpected inexpensive like sort of commodity product and really a direct along Facebook ad. You need something to talk about. And you need something you need differentiators. You're giving people reasons to be interested in the buy. So if it's a $2 flashlight, well, you know, I don't know. And you can do it. The tactical flashlight guys are doing it. It's their front end product. And I had no idea you could say as much as you can say about a flashlight. So it really comes down to like, do you need? How many words do you need to sell the thing that you're selling? Like to explain the benefits. I'm guessing you don't need a multi page presale site. So you're going direct to E-commerce. But be creative to, you know, there's sort of that story, which he's confirmed. Mike Rowe, the guy from Dirty Jobs, a really fascinating guy. Mike Rowe, Rowe started his career as a sales guy, late night sales guy in QVC. And his interview was to sell a pencil. He just sat across the table from somebody, they had him in regular number two pencil and said, sell a pencil. So, you know, you can't get pretty creative in your writing. But the ad really should be a reflection of what there is to talk about the product without getting into the micro-selling a pencil kind of thing. Like that gets a little like, that's an interesting exercise, but it's not going to take your audience long to figure out if you're selling a $2 flashlight in sort of crazy, complicated ways. So not knowing the product makes us hard to answer, but the broader answer is the amount of copy in your ad should be reflective of what it takes to pre-qualify a good candidate. And if you are selling a $2 flashlight and that, and it's, it appeals to a sort of a tactical audience that's got the cool clip that does the thing in the light, that does the other thing, and you can bash something in the head with it because it's pointed, you can probably cover that in 250, 300 words. And don't talk about bashing something in the head with the flashlight on Facebook. Lauren will probably tell you that too. But it just, it just needs to reflect what would make somebody realize, hey, this thing is for me or I have no interest in that. Like I want my flashlights to be friendlier. I don't know. Lauren, thoughts on this? I think if speed of implementation is an issue, if just climbing that hill is an issue, start with the minimal viable funnel and move from there. And that sounds like it may be pretty easy for you to do a long form Facebook ad to go to the page, see how that works, and then later implement in the NPPS and then test and see which one performs better. That's always the answer. Test, test, test, test. We don't know. Nobody knows. You know, the data, the data knows. And you just try stuff and understand what you tried and understand the differences between the things you tried and then let the data tell you what works. And it might not work the same in a month. Might not work the same in a week. Just testing this for a part of the deal. And that's module six that we're working on beyond this week is about that. Like how do you think about that? All right. So having said that little side public service announcement, I have one foundational question I have, I think is good content on a soap opera sequence from TLB content. I am now building an ad and two times multi-page pre-sale content. So two pages maybe to sit before this. Okay. So if I'm reading this correctly, we've got two page multi-page pre-sale site and a soap opera sequence. Those two are connected. How do I use that content over three contact touchpoints? Do I spread it out across all three? Then an ad, another in a multi-page pre-sale site and the rest in this. So, okay, I see where we're going. And do I use the ideas three times and overlap the ad and multi-page pre-sale site in the soap opera sequence, reiterating the idea three times. So maybe reinforcing it. So really interesting questions embedded in this, right? Do we, if we think about an ad, a multi-page pre-sale site and a soap opera sequence, those three things are all part of one large narrative arc. Right. So if we were to look at it from the proper perspective, we would see that it's all really one long thing and that one long thing leads to the sale at the end. Right. So the way I would approach this, and I think this will be interesting for all three of us to answer, but here's how I would approach it. And this is, and when I say would approach it, this is how I have done exactly this dozens of times working with clients. I go to the end, borrow a page from Steven Covey, begin with the end in mind. And I ask myself, what does somebody need to believe in order to make this whatever the offer is at the end? Now, of course, those of you who have purchased Sphere of Influence know that this is, this is directly from Sphere of Influence, this chain of beliefs. But I go all the way to the end when I have revealed that there is something for sale. And I then ask myself, just brainstorm. I go through the exact process that Andre listed in Sphere of Influence. And I say, okay, what, what are all the beliefs? And I just get them out of my head. And what I find after I get them all out of my head is that I have described the same belief three or four different ways often. So my next part of the process is I go through what I think is 20 beliefs to find it's usually like nine or something. So I combine the right things and I get the language right. I don't spend a ton of time word smithing this, but I get, I get the. So that I know that these are discrete sets of beliefs. I get them all on a page. Once I have them all on the page, I then sequence them. What I'm looking for is the, the primary belief, the thing that others call this a marketing thesis. I, for me, it is, it's sort of that turning point belief. Like, what does this all need to roll up into that somebody needs to accept before they can make this purchase that I know I'm going to reveal because I can see the whole narrative arc from 10,000 feet. So every once I have that list and I have it sequenced, and I, I then go and look across the breadth of the ad, the ad needs to cover, you know, the ad is covering a certain, it's covering sort of more ground because it's hinting at several of these beliefs. It's opening up lots of these ideas that gets them sort of engaged in it. So it's not just a linear one belief in the ad, one belief, you know, in the first page of the presale site. You have to, on the front, kind of create a little intro, a little act one overview, set the stage of what's to come. It's like any good movie that does this, you know, it's sort of, you introduce the idea and you kind of give a sense of, you know, with the music and some other things like where, where's this thing going? Is it comedy? Is it drama? Is it horror? Like, well, what's the vibe here? Right. So that's, that's the ad does that the ad gets into the multi-page presale site, the multi-page presale site and auto responder madness and inspired soap opera sequence are building chains of beliefs in approximately the order that I have identified so that when the person has consumed all of that, I know they have been exposed to that they're, they, I have reinforced, I've created a world that has, that has answered and established and helped them internalize the beliefs that they need to have so that when the product is revealed, they believe what they, the right person believes what he or she needs to believe to accept the offer that I've made. And if they're not, then I know I have not, I didn't, I haven't covered all the beliefs, but that's what I'm looking for in that process. So Andre thoughts on that? Have the same, same idea, how you approach that? Yeah. I mean, that's, that's pretty much it. I think, you know, there's, there's some ideas that's also worth bearing in mind that, you know, if, if somebody clicks on an ad and reads the two or three pages of a, of a multi-page and then they opt in for the email sequence, well, you've already done many things right because you've got the attention, you've, you've intrigued them enough and you've given them enough value and you have the attention to the point where they've really enabled to opt in and to continue this journey. So that's the one thing. The other thing is, as we know, you can't convert everybody. So there's going to be the majority of people that just don't bar. Um, and then you can just have, have fun with the email sequence, right? So there's so many different ways to, to talk about ideas and to frame concepts and emails perfect because it's, it's very conversational, um, one to one. And you get to play around with that a bit. Um, so, you know, you could, you could test one, one idea across two or three emails and see how that works. You could then write another sequence and then just bought that on. So, um, at the point where somebody opts in, um, it's almost like an A, B split test where some go into the one sequence and the others go into the other sequence. And then you kind of see which sequence does a better job. If, if you wanted to create, make it longer, you could then certainly have these, these storylines that play out through one sequence. Obviously when, uh, when somebody makes a purchase, they, they're removed from the sequence entirely. Um, you know, there's, there's no hard and fast rule other than, um, if you can't get somebody to opt in, there's something, there's something broken or not right. Um, further upstream could be the ad, um, could be the targeting, could be all the way, you know, right at the very top. Um, and then you just got to figure it out as, as each point in the process, you get people or enough people at macro scale or whatever you feel is a, is a win. And then you can just keep moving further, further downstream. But as, as Sean said, it's important to figure out what those, you know, what those beliefs are, what somebody needs to believe in order to accept whatever you're selling. Um, and then you just get to tell different stories within that, that narrative arc and because there's no, there's no one story that's going to rule them all. You know, everybody responds to different things. Everybody's intrigued by different ideas. And yeah, so he's got to grow that. Hey, Lawrence, you noticed there when Andre went to the blame game, the first thing he blame was the ad. I felt that. How's that? That's starting a little bit. You used to use to that. Yeah. Right. Exactly. So this is a, this is outside of sort of specific to Facebook. Learned by love to hear your thoughts on this too, because you write, um, you've, you've written a lot of long form Facebook copy. There's an art to it. Um, a little bit of science, but far more art. So when you sit down to create that initial entry into a, a longer, broader world, how do you think about that? Um, if I think that would be, they, having you through discuss that, how you approach it would be very valuable to people listening. I, I, I place a big emphasis on transitions. And the, the key to a good long form Facebook ad is probably very similar to the entire funnel from ad to, to the, you know, multi-page pre-sell to the soap opera sequence. Um, every point should just flow naturally to where people get to the ends and they're, they're kind of like, Oh, well, I was just reading this entire time because this was great. Like I don't even realize what beliefs I have, uh, I have accepted now along the, along this journey. Um, it's a journey that is, that is pleasurable. And I spend a lot of time making sure that every single part of that Facebook ad flows, there's going to be, uh, some, some natural times when the transition might be more difficult. Um, when you start, you may start off telling an emotionally gripping story. And then maybe you move into describing how your product works. Uh, maybe you, you transition into a call to action. Um, what, maybe that's direct to sale or, or click over to this, uh, whatever is, you know, you're, you're, you're selling on a page one of the multi-page pre-sell, whatever you, you, you want the payoff to be there. Um, but just smoothing out as much as possible, those, uh, those transition points. That can be very tactical sometimes. It could be even little tricks like, um, a two word phrase that just says, now look, and, and, you know, and, and then you, you move into the rest of your, your, your ad, uh, whatever you can do to arrest people's attention again, to keep them moving, to keep things flowing, to keep it conversational. And I think that philosophy could extend over the course of your entire, uh, your entire funnel setup. There's going to be natural points at which that transition is difficult. Going from the Facebook ad over to the first page of the multi, uh, the multi-page pre-sell, that's going to be a difficult transition point. So what can you do to make that smooth? What can you do to entice people to take that leap? Um, and then what payoff are they getting, uh, quickly to justify them taking that action so that they continue to take actions and just think about that for every single, uh, transition point. Of course, clicking to that next page in a multi-page pre-sell, um, moving, entering in your, uh, your contact information at the end, opening up the, each email moving on, just think of, um, what are your transition points and what can you do to, to maximize the flow through your entire funnel? Because if someone just reads your Facebook ad and they never click over, you know, that's, you just wasted everything. If they read your ad and they only make it to the second page of your multi-page pre-sell and they never put in their information, then, uh, you know, what was the point of it all? So you've got to, got to just keep them flowing the whole time. Appreciate that, Lauren.
